speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape six. The date is January 16, 1997. I am conducting and in interview.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tape seven.

speaker: interviewee
text: It's tape seven.

speaker: interviewer
text: Sorry.

speaker: interviewee
text: Tape seven.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can I start again? This is tape seven. The date is January 16, 1997. I'm conducting an interview with Barbara [? Marlowe. ?] Location is London, England.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we are now into 1954. We are both working. I decided to go into market research. And my, I got my first job with [? McClain's ?] of the Beecham Group. Then I changed jobs and worked for Gillette Industries up to 1957, when I had to give up work because of a difficult pregnancy with my first daughter. Monica was born on the 14th of November, 1957. And a year later, I returned to the work as an assistant to the economic adviser at Lloyds Bank, where I stayed for the next three years until the birth of my second daughter, Joanna, on the 5th of February, 1962. I then decided that, although I liked my professional work, life was too complicated with two young children. And I started working freelance while at Lloyds Bank, part of the department involved publication of Lloyds Bank review. And I had already been asked to do some charts for a newly-formed Institute of Economic Affairs and for a couple of other magazines. So I decided to concentrate on my freelance work and not to work full-time. And this worked quite well. At one stage, I was doing charts for newspaper, till the trade unions got fed up and decided that I wasn't a member and therefore, started blacking out my work-- similar problems with But I got more and more contacts. And through my work with the Institute of Economic Affairs, I was asked to do a book for them, a book of charts on the British economy discussing the different sources of statistics and so on. And that was published in 1964 by Longman. I did a bit of writing for other people, Worker's Education Association, something for [INAUDIBLE]. And then I was approached by a small consultancy and asked whether I would like to do some work for them on a freelance basis, which I did. And this sort of grew and grew over time, so that I did quite a lot of work for them. And up to this time, I'm still doing bits and pieces. It also enabled me to do some traveling to Brazil, to the United States. [PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: We can do, yeah. [INAUDIBLE] [? stop there. ?] Carry on [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes? And in Europe, so that I have been lucky enough to be able to combine family life with professional work, which I enjoy. And I feel I need. Sadly, my mother died in 1977, and my father four years later. But he had the pleasure of seeing my elder daughter married, although he didn't see the birth of his -great grandchildren. Monica graduated from [INAUDIBLE] University in biochemistry and genetics, and followed this with a PhD in biochemistry, which she got in 1984. Sadly, she developed multiply sclerosis, which has advanced very rapidly over the last couple of years. Her marriage broke up, and she is now still struggling to keep her children with her, although this is becoming increasingly difficult. She's got two children, James who is 11 and 1/2, and Georgina, named after my brother, who will be eight next month, in February. Joanna originally planned to study architecture but changed her mind and did a general social science degree at Edinburgh University, after which she worked very successfully as a planner in leading advertising agencies. She married in 1990. And her first child was born in May, 1991. William is now five and 1/2. She's followed with two more in quick succession, Annabelle in 1982, and Emily in 1995. So I now have five grandchildren. And the joy of watching them grow, though tinged with sadness that a way, I will probably lose my two elder ones when their father decides that it's time for him to assume responsibility for them. Sometimes I think that life is very unfair, that after all the problems and difficulties of my early life, I should have been able to look forward to a less stressful old age. But then, that's part of life.

speaker: interviewer
text: Barbara, I wanted to ask you if there is any message, any special words that you'd like to, to say at this stage about your life and how it's affected you and what you really think as a result of it?

speaker: interviewee
text: It may sound very strange, but I feel that I wouldn't want to change anything. I think I have learned an awful lot from what I went through and problem-- I don't know what I can say-- it's a question of judgment, but a better person than I would have been. I have more insight, more understanding, more appreciation of the values. That's why I think education was something that was extremely important to me, both for myself and for my family. And it remains, in my view, the most important thin and the only thing you can give to your children as a basis for their future. Everything else is irrelevant. Money, possessions, they, they're really not very important. But education is something which stays with you for life. I'm proud of the achievement of my family, past, present, and future. And if I was to summarize what I feel, I think I would go back to that poem, which I quoted before, which says that faith, instead of uniting, divides. And I would like to see this reversed. When I look at today's world, I see how very true this is in every context. And I think the most important thing is that people should learn to live together, to look at each other as people, and not in terms of religion.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Barbara, what you tell me about this document?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a certified copy of a trading register, which shows the transfer of ownership from my father to [? Aryan ?] liquidators. When Jews were no longer allowed to have a business, that was a way of getting, setting up the liquidation of the business itself.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me the date that this was issued?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I think the main, the important entry was in 1939, '40.

speaker: interviewer
text: Inside of the document?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, basically, just says that who the owners of the business were and who took over. The, the first liquidator died. And he was replaced by somebody else. And this is entered on this register. But it was a liquidation as a result of the German occupation.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Can you tell me the names of each of the people in this photograph?

speaker: interviewee
text: This photograph shows three generations of the Bregman family. My uncle, Alexander Bregman, is the first one on the right. Next to him is Sebastian Bregman, my great-grandfather. The next one is Dr. Ludwik Bregman, my grandfather, who was consultant neurologist and head of the department at the [INAUDIBLE] Hospital in Warsaw. And the last one is my father, Leon Bregman.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you know the date this photograph was taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. It must-- it was probably in the mid 1930s.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was it taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: Somewhere near, near Warsaw, I would say.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Can you tell me the names of the people in this photograph?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my grandmother.

speaker: interviewer
text: And her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Ika Bregman, with me. So it must have been taken in 1932.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was it taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: Somewhere on holiday, by the look, or in the park-- either in a park in Warsaw or somewhere on holiday.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. And the name of this woman?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my mother, Bronislawa Bregman. Photograph was taken in 1942 and was used for her identity document, the Kennkarte, which was issued by the Germans. And on the back of this photograph is her wartime name, the one she used in hiding, [? Paulina Charteska ?].

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you tell me the name of the young girl here?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's myself at the age of 11 in the Warsaw ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my photograph from my school past, taken in 1943 when I was using my assumed name of [? Maria Namnishka ?]. You can still see the stamps, the German stamps, on this photograph.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who's this?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's my photograph at the end of the war. I'm wearing the blouse given by the Russian liberators, and must have been early, mid 1945.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was it taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think probably in Lodz.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you give me the name of this young man?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a photograph of my brother, George, who was missing in action with the Polish air force on the 19th of July, 1944.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was this taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This would have been taken in England. But I don't know where, whether it was at his station, or--

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a tombstone at the Jewish cemetery in Warsaw, which has been replaced by my father and bears the names of the most immediate members of the family, my great-grandfather, Sebastian Bregman, my grandfather, Ludwik Bregman, who are buried at the cemetery, my grandmother, Ika Bregman, who was murdered in Treblinka, in 1942, and my brother, George Jerzy Bregman, who was missing in action. It-- also mentioned is my father's sister, who died in infancy and whose grave couldn't be identified after the Germans had destroyed the cemetery. This is a new tombstone, as shown in the previous photograph, but showing how the Germans have destroyed all the tombs in that area in the cemetery.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you name people in this photograph?

speaker: interviewee
text: On the right, you have my daughter, Monica. Next to her is my mother, Bronislawa Bregman, myself standing behind her, my father on the left, and my youngest daughter, Joanna, in front. The picture was taken in 1974 in our garden in London.

speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewer
text: Monica is the dark haired?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: On the left.

speaker: interviewer
text: You said, you said on the right.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah. Sorry.

speaker: interviewer
text: What should we do? Should we do it again? Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: We should do it again.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you tell me the names of the people in this photograph?

speaker: interviewee
text: On the right is Monica.

speaker: interviewer
text: Left,

speaker: interviewer
text: This is Monica, yes?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. That's on the right.

speaker: interviewer
text: So looking at it, it's on the left. If we're looking at it face on--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh.

speaker: interviewer
text: Sorry.

speaker: interviewee
text: All right. Sorry.

speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewer
text: It's All right.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that it appears on this [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. On the left is Monica. Next to her is my mother, Bronislawa Bregman, myself standing behind, my father, Leon Bregman, on the left, and my younger daughter, Joanna, on the ground at their feet. The photograph was taken in 1974 in our garden in London.
